24: oikodomesen — built — the aorist indicates completed action, the wise builder actually builds (vs. merely hearing). akouon mou tous logous toutous kai poion autous — hearing my words and doing them — the two-stage requirement mirrors the Shema (shema = hear and obey). petra — bedrock, solid rock — contrasted with psammos (sand); the Semitic image of the rock as foundation for covenant stability (Isa 28:16, the tested cornerstone in Zion). The house built on rock withstands floods and winds — the twin disasters of Palestinian flash floods and seasonal windstorms.
1: mē krinete — do not judge (krinō, present imperative: stop the ongoing practice) — the prohibition is not against all moral evaluation (v.6 requires discriminating judgment about dogs and pigs) but against the presumptuous, condemnatory judgment that sets oneself up as a final tribunal over another. Hina mē krithēte — so that you will not be judged — purpose clause: the prohibition has a self-interested logic as well as a moral one. The passive krithēte (be judged) is a divine passive: God is the agent of the judgment returned upon the judge.
2: hō gar krimati krinete krithēsesthe — for with what judgment you judge, you will be judged — the sentence is an antanaclasis: the same word-root (krin-) in three forms (noun, verb, passive future) applied to the one doing the judging. Kai en hō metrō metreite metrēthēsetai hymin — and with the measure you measure, it will be measured to you — metreō/metron (to measure/measure): the commercial imagery of weights and scales applied to moral accountability. The passive metrēthēsetai is again divine passive.
3: to karphos to en tō ophthalmō tou adelphou sou — the speck (karphos, a tiny splinter, chip of wood or straw) in your brother's eye — karphos is the minimum visible fragment. Tēn de en tō sō ophthalmō dokon — the plank (dokos, a load-bearing beam) in your own eye — the absurdist hyperbole inverts perception: one who cannot see because of a beam in their own eye judges the vision of someone with a splinter. The contrast is not merely one of scale but of self-knowledge.
4: pōs ereis tō adelphō sou — how can you say to your brother — pōs introduces a rhetorical impossibility: the question is not "will you say this" but "how can you say this." Aphes ekbalō to karphos ek tou ophthalmou sou — let me take the speck out of your eye — aphes aorist imperative of aphiēmi: let me remove it; the volunteer helper asks permission, all the while blind to their own larger obstruction. Kai idou hē dokos en tō ophthalmō sou — and look, the plank is in your own eye.
5: hypokrita — hypocrite — the same word used for religious performers in 6:2,5,16; here applied to the self-deceived judge who sees clearly abroad but not at home. Ekbale prōton ek tou ophthalmou sou tēn dokon — first take the plank out of your own eye — prōton (first) is the structural principle: self-examination precedes correction of others. Kai tote diablepseis ekbalein to karphos ek tou ophthalmou tou adelphou sou — and then you will see clearly (diablepō, to see through/clearly) to remove the speck from your brother's eye.
6: mē dōte to hagion tois kysin — do not give what is holy to dogs — to hagion (the holy thing, the sacred) and tous margaritas (the pearls) are paired as objects of wisdom-teaching. Kysin (dogs) and tois choirois (pigs) were both ritually unclean animals in Jewish law and metaphors for those who treat sacred things with contempt. Mēpote katapatesousin autous en tois posin autōn kai straphentes rhēxōsin hymas — lest they trample them underfoot and turning tear you to pieces — the reversal: instead of receiving the gift, they destroy both it and the giver. The verse requires discrimination in sharing sacred wisdom, balancing 7:1 which prohibits judicial arrogance.
7: aiteite kai dothēsetai hymin — ask and it will be given to you — three present imperatives in sequence: aiteite (ask), zēteite (seek), krouete (knock). The present tense marks all three as ongoing, persistent activities, not one-time requests. Dothēsetai (it will be given) — future passive divine. The progression from asking to seeking to knocking may indicate increasing urgency or effort, or it may be a rhetorical triplet expressing the same reality three ways.
8: pas gar ho aitōn lambanei — for everyone who asks receives — the three present active participles (ho aitōn/ho zētōn/ho krouōn) are universal: every one who asks... every one who seeks... every one who knocks. Lambanei (receives), heuriskei (finds), and the passive anoigēsetai (it will be opened) match the three actions of v.7. The movement from imperative to indicative (from command to promise) grounds the exhortation in certainty of outcome.
9: ē tis estin ex hymōn anthrōpos hon aitēsei ho huios autou arton — or who among you is a person whose son asks for bread — a rhetorical question expecting "no one." Arton... lithon — bread... stone: similar in shape (round flat bread resembling a flat stone of the region). The question is about the father's response: would a father deliberately give what harms when asked for what nourishes? The argument moves from human paternal instinct to divine generosity.
10: ē kai ichthyn aitēsei — or if he asks for a fish — ichthyn (fish) and ophīn (snake) are paired as the second analogy: both elongated, similar in shape. Mē ophin epidōsei autō — will he not give him a snake? — mē introduces a question expecting "no" (negative answer). The two analogies (bread/stone, fish/snake) establish by everyday human parental behavior the principle that guides the divine argument in v.11.
11: ei oun hymeis ponēroi ontes — if then you, being evil — ponēroi ontes: present active participle acknowledging the moral condition of the disciples without softening it; the argument explicitly includes human sinfulness as the baseline from which the a fortiori (poso mallon, how much more) operates. Oidate domata agatha didonai tois teknois hymōn — know how to give good gifts to your children — oidate (perfect-as-present: you already know). Poso mallon ho patēr hymōn ho en tois ouranois dōsei agatha tois aitousin auton — how much more will your heavenly Father give good things to those who ask him.
12: panta oun hosa ean thelēte hina poiōsin hymin hoi anthrōpoi — everything therefore that you want people to do to you — oun (therefore) connects the Golden Rule to what preceded: the character of the heavenly Father (v.11) who gives good things is the ground for treating others as you wish to be treated. Houtōs kai hymeis poieite autois — so also do to them. Houtos gar estin ho nomos kai hoi prophētai — for this is the Law and the Prophets — the same phrase as 5:17; the Golden Rule is not a replacement of the Torah but its concentrated summary (cf. Rom 13:9-10, Gal 5:14).
13: eiselthate dia tēs stenēs pylēs — enter through the narrow gate (eiselthate, aorist imperative: a decisive act of entering) — stenē (narrow, constricted) and plateia (wide, broad) are contrasted. Hoti plateia hē pylē kai eurychōros hē hodos hē apagousa eis tēn apōleian — for wide is the gate and spacious the road leading to destruction (apōleia, loss/ruin/perdition). Kai polloi eisin hoi eiserchomenoi di autēs — and many are those entering through it — polloi (many) is not comforting here; popularity is not a mark of the correct path.
14: hoti stenē hē pylē kai tethlimmenē hē hodos — for narrow is the gate and constricted (thlibō, to press, squeeze) the road — tethlimmenē perfect passive participle: the road has been narrowed, pressed in from both sides; it is not merely small but actively constrained. Hē apagousa eis tēn zōēn — leading to life — zōē (life) is the counterpart to apōleia (destruction) in v.13. Kai oligoi eisin hoi heuriskontes autēn — and few are those who find it — oligoi (few): the road is so narrow it must be actively found (heuriskontes, present participle: those who are in the process of finding).
15: prosechete apo tōn pseudoprophētōn — beware of the false prophets (prosechō apo + genitive: to guard oneself from, hold back from) — pseudoprophētai are not simply inaccurate predictors but those who claim prophetic authority while serving false ends. Hoitines erchontai pros hymas en endymasin probatōn — who come to you in sheep's clothing (endymasin, outer garments, what one is dressed in) — the garb of the sheep: the appearance of belonging to the flock. Esōthen de eisin lykoi harpages — but inwardly they are rapacious wolves (harpax, seizing, grasping, predatory).
16: apo tōn karpōn autōn epignōsesthe autous — by their fruits you will recognize them (epiginōskō, to know thoroughly, recognize fully — the epi- prefix intensifying the knowing) — the criterion is practical and observable: not their words, charisma, or claimed credentials, but their karpoi (fruits, products, outcomes). Mēti syllegousin apo akanthōn staphylas ē apo tribōlōn syka — do people gather grapes from thornbushes or figs from thistles? — a rhetorical question establishing that plant-type determines fruit-type absolutely.
17: pan dendron agathon karpous kalous poiei — every good tree makes good fruit — agathos (intrinsically good, good in nature) vs. sapros (rotten, decayed, spoiled: used for rotting wood and bad fish, Matt 13:48). The logic is ontological: what a thing is determines what it produces. To de sapron dendron karpous ponērous poiei — but the rotten tree makes bad/evil fruit — ponērous (bad, harmful, evil): the same word as "the evil one" in 5:37 and "evil people" in 7:11.
18: ou dynatai dendron agathon karpous ponērous poiein — a good tree cannot bear bad fruit — ou dynatai (is not able): the logical impossibility is stated as an ontological incapacity, not merely an improbability. Oude dendron sapron karpous kalous poiein — nor can a rotten tree bear good fruit — the symmetrical impossibility stated in both directions. The botanical logic grounds the identification of false prophets in character rather than in claimed criteria.
19: pan dendron mē poioun karpon kalon ekkoptetai kai eis pyr balletai — every tree not bearing good fruit is cut down and thrown into fire — ekkoptetai (present passive: is being cut down) and balletai (present passive: is being thrown into fire) — the present tenses may be gnomic (general truth) or proleptic (anticipating the certain future). The language echoes John the Baptist's preaching (3:10: "even now the axe is laid at the root of the trees"). The eschatological fire is the endpoint of fruitlessness.
20: ara ge apo tōn karpōn autōn epignōsesthe autous — therefore by their fruits you will recognize them — a near-verbatim repetition of v.16b, forming an inclusio that frames the tree/fruit argument of vv.16-19. Ara ge is an emphatic inferential particle: "therefore indeed," drawing the conclusion firmly. The repetition drives the diagnostic criterion home: fruits are the test, not claims of authority, spiritual experience, or formal credentials.
21: ou pas ho legōn moi kyrie kyrie eiseleusetai eis tēn basileian tōn ouranōn — not everyone who says to me "Lord, Lord" will enter the kingdom of heaven — ou pas... all: the distributive negation singles out a subclass of "Lord-sayers" who will not enter. Kyrie kyrie — the doubled address is a Semitic intensifier (cf. "Absalom, Absalom," "Martha, Martha") indicating earnestness or familiarity; but familiarity with the formula does not constitute relationship. Ho de poiōn to thelēma tou patros mou tou en ouranois — but the one doing the will of my Father in heaven — the present active participle marks ongoing, habitual doing.
22: polloi erousin moi en ekeinē tē hēmera — many will say to me on that day — ekeinē tē hēmera (that day) is the day of final judgment; the plural polloi warns that this is not an edge case. Kyrie kyrie ou tō sō onomati eprophēteusamen kai tō sō onomati daimonia exebalomen kai tō sō onomati dynamous pollas epoiēsamen — Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name, and in your name drive out demons, and in your name perform many miracles? — three extraordinary religious acts all performed en tō sō onomati (in your name): real acts, apparently, but performed without genuine relationship.
23: kai tote homologēsō autois hoti oudepote egnōn hymas — and then I will declare to them: I never knew you — homologeō (to confess, declare openly): the declaration is public and definitive. Oudepote egnōn hymas — I never knew you — oudepote (never at any time) + egnōn (aorist of ginōskō): I knew you not, not even once. Ginōskō in Hebrew thought denotes relational, covenantal knowing (cf. Amos 3:2, "you only have I known" = chosen in covenant relationship). The issue is not information but relationship. Apochōreite ap’ emou hoi ergazomenoi tēn anomian — depart from me you workers of lawlessness (anomia, lawlessness, the opposite of the law-fulfilling righteousness Jesus taught in ch5–6).
25: kai katebē hē brochē kai ēlthon hoi potamoi kai epneusan hoi anemoi — the rain came down and the rivers came and the winds blew — three agents of destruction in sequence: rain (brochē), flood-streams (potamoi), winds (anemoi). Kai prosepesan tē oikia ekeinē — and beat against that house — prosepiptō (to fall against, rush against). Kai ouk epesen — and it did not fall — aorist negated: a completed test with a completed non-collapse. Tetheliouto gar epi tēn petran — for it had been founded on the rock — pluperfect passive: the foundation pre-existed the storm and its condition determined the outcome.
26: pas ho akouōn mou tous logous toutous kai mē poiōn autous — everyone who hears these words of mine and does not do them — the two-part condition: hearing is present (ongoing), not-doing is also present (habitual omission). The contrast with v.24 is grammatically precise: v.24 has akouōn... kai poiōn (hearing and doing); v.26 has akouōn... kai mē poiōn (hearing and not-doing). Homoiōthēsetai andri mōrō — will be like a foolish man (mōros, the same root as morainō, to become foolish, in 5:13). The foolishness is not intellectual deficiency but the moral failure to act on what one has received.
27: kai katebē hē brochē kai ēlthon hoi potamoi kai epneusan hoi anemoi — identical storm-formula to v.25 — the same three agents strike the same way. Kai prosekospan tē oikia ekeinē — and struck against that house — proskoptō (to strike against, stumble into) vs. v.25's prosepiptō; both convey the force of the assault. Kai epesen kai ēn hē ptōsis autēs megalē — and it fell, and great was its fall — ptōsis (fall, collapse): the noun from piptō. The greatness of the fall measures the greatness of the investment in false foundation; the more one builds on sand, the more catastrophic the collapse.
28: kai egeneto hote etelesen ho Iēsous tous logous toutous — and it came to pass when Jesus finished these words — egeneto hote is a Matthean formula (also 11:1; 13:53; 19:1; 26:1) marking the end of the five great discourses of Matthew's Gospel. Etelesen aorist of teleō: completed, finished — the same root as teleios (mature, complete) of 5:48. Exeplessonto hoi ochloi epi tē didachē autou — the crowds were astonished (ekplēssō, to strike out of one's wits, astonish thoroughly) at his teaching.
29: ēn gar didaskōn autous hōs exousian echōn — for he was teaching them as one having authority (exousia, the right and power to act, legitimate authority from one's own standing) — hōs exousian echōn: "as" implies manner but also content; the comparison identifies his mode of speaking. Kai ouch hōs hoi grammateis autōn — and not as their scribes — the scribes taught by citing tradition and precedent (the rabbis say..., it has been transmitted that..., the school of Hillel holds..., the school of Shammai holds...); Jesus teaches on his own authority: "but I tell you" (5:22, 28, 32, 34, 39, 44).
